_meta.source: Compiled from PlantVillage dataset documentation, ICAR plant pathology guidelines, and agricultural extension resources

Apple___Apple_scab.name: Apple Scab
Apple___Apple_scab.description: Apple scab is a serious fungal disease that causes dark, olive-green to black lesions on leaves and fruit. It overwinters in fallen infected leaves and releases spores in spring during wet weather, leading to primary infections on new foliage.
causes: Fungal spores (Venturia inaequalis) overwintering in fallen leaves
Prolonged wet, cool weather (15-25°C) during spring
Poor air circulation in dense canopies
Overhead irrigation creating leaf wetness
Lack of resistant cultivars
symptoms: Olive-green to dark brown spots on leaves
Velvety or scabby lesions on fruit surface
Deformed or cracked fruit
Premature leaf drop
Reduced fruit size and quality
chemical_treatment: Captan (contact fungicide) — apply at green tip stage
Myclobutanil (systemic fungicide) — during active infection
Mancozeb — preventive application during wet periods
eco_friendly_treatment: Neem oil spray (2-3%) — natural fungicide with minimal residue
Bordeaux mixture (copper sulfate + lime) — traditional organic fungicide
Potassium bicarbonate solution — disrupts fungal cell walls
Trichoderma-based biocontrol agents — competitive colonizers
Compost tea foliar spray — promotes beneficial microbes on leaf surface
Apple___Apple_scab.nutritional_impact: Moderate — infected fruit may show reduced vitamin C content (up to 15% decrease) and lower sugar accumulation. Heavily scabbed fruit has lower market value but internal nutrition remains largely unaffected if disease is superficial.
best_practices: Remove and destroy fallen infected leaves in autumn to reduce spore source
Plant scab-resistant varieties (Liberty, Enterprise, Freedom, Redfree)
Maintain open canopy through proper pruning for air circulation
Avoid overhead sprinklers; use drip irrigation instead
Apply preventive fungicide before rain during susceptible growth stages
Monitor weather forecasts and use disease prediction models for spray timing
prevention: Choose resistant cultivars when planting new orchards
Regularly prune to improve air flow
Rake and compost fallen leaves to reduce inoculum

Corn_(maize)___Common_rust.name: Common Rust
Corn_(maize)___Common_rust.description: Common rust of corn is caused by the fungus Puccinia sorghi and is characterized by small, reddish-brown pustules on both leaf surfaces. The disease is most prevalent during moderate temperatures and high humidity, and spores can travel long distances via wind.
causes: Wind-borne fungal spores from southern regions or alternate hosts
Moderate temperatures (16-23°C) with high humidity
Extended dew periods on leaf surfaces
Planting susceptible hybrids
Late planting that extends exposure during favorable disease conditions
symptoms: Small, circular to elongated reddish-brown pustules on leaves
Pustules appear on both upper and lower leaf surfaces
Heavily infected leaves may turn yellow and dry out
Reduction in photosynthetic area
In severe cases, pustules appear on husks and leaf sheaths
chemical_treatment: Azoxystrobin (strobilurin fungicide) — systemic, broad-spectrum
Propiconazole (triazole fungicide) — curative action
Tebuconazole — preventive and curative
eco_friendly_treatment: Neem seed kernel extract (NSKE 5%) — reduces spore germination
Panchagavya spray (3%) — enhances plant immunity naturally
Silicon-based foliar spray — strengthens cell walls against penetration
Bacillus subtilis-based biocontrol — competitive exclusion of pathogen
Garlic extract spray — natural antifungal properties
Corn_(maize)___Common_rust.nutritional_impact: Low to moderate — reduces grain filling efficiency by 5-15% in moderate infections. Protein content may decrease slightly in severely affected ears. Starch content largely unchanged for mild to moderate infections.
best_practices: Plant rust-resistant hybrids (most modern hybrids have Rp resistance genes)
Avoid late planting to minimize exposure during peak spore season
Scout fields regularly from tasseling onward
Apply fungicide when pustules first appear on lower leaves
Rotate crops to reduce local inoculum buildup
Maintain balanced fertilization — avoid excessive nitrogen
prevention: Select hybrids with known rust resistance genes
Plant at recommended dates for your region
Maintain proper plant spacing for air circulation
Avoid excessive nitrogen which increases susceptibility

Grape___Black_rot.name: Black Rot
Grape___Black_rot.description: Grape black rot is a devastating fungal disease that can destroy an entire crop if left unmanaged. It causes circular brown lesions on leaves, dark sunken cankers on shoots, and eventually converts green berries into hard, black, shriveled mummies.
causes: Fungal spores released from mummified berries during warm, wet spring weather
Extended periods of leaf wetness (6+ hours at 21-27°C)
Poor vineyard sanitation — old mummies left on vines or ground
Dense canopy restricting air movement
Heavy rainfall during bloom and fruit development
symptoms: Circular reddish-brown spots on leaves with dark borders
Dark pycnidia (fruiting bodies) visible in leaf spots
Infected berries develop light brown rot that spreads rapidly
Berries shrivel into hard, black, wrinkled mummies
Dark cankers on infected shoots
chemical_treatment: Mancozeb — protective, apply before infection periods
Captan — broad-spectrum contact fungicide
Myclobutanil — systemic with curative activity
eco_friendly_treatment: Bordeaux mixture (copper sulfate + lime) — traditional organic option
Sulfur dust or wettable sulfur spray — effective in dry conditions
Neem oil (2%) — suppresses fungal sporulation
Bacillus amyloliquefaciens foliar spray — colonizes leaves, out-competes fungus
Compost extract spray — introduces beneficial microbiome to leaf surface
Grape___Black_rot.nutritional_impact: High — severely infected berries are unmarketable. Sugar content (Brix) drops significantly in partially affected clusters. Wine quality from affected grapes is poor with off-flavors. Healthy berries on partially affected clusters maintain normal nutrition.
best_practices: Remove and destroy all mummified fruit from vines and ground before bud break
Apply fungicide sprays from pre-bloom through 4-6 weeks post-bloom
Train vines for open canopy and good air circulation
Remove leaves around fruit clusters to speed drying
Cultivate soil to bury fallen mummies and reduce spore release
Use disease forecasting models based on temperature and wetness duration
prevention: Thorough winter sanitation — remove all mummies
Maintain open canopy through summer pruning
Select less susceptible varieties if available
Manage vineyard floor to decompose fallen debris

Potato___Early_blight.name: Early Blight
Potato___Early_blight.description: Early blight of potato is one of the most common foliar diseases worldwide. It causes characteristic concentric-ring 'target spot' lesions on lower leaves first, progressively moving upward. The disease reduces photosynthetic area and can significantly reduce tuber yield.
causes: Fungal spores (Alternaria solani) surviving in soil and plant debris
Alternating wet and dry periods with temperatures of 25-30°C
Nutritional stress, especially nitrogen or potassium deficiency
Aging plant tissue — older plants more susceptible
Consecutive potato cropping in same field without rotation
symptoms: Dark brown, circular lesions with concentric rings (target spots) on lower leaves
Yellow halo around lesions
Lesions may coalesce, causing entire leaves to wither
Disease progresses from lower to upper leaves
Tubers may develop dark, sunken surface lesions
chemical_treatment: Chlorothalonil — broad-spectrum protectant, apply preventively
Mancozeb — contact fungicide, preventive applications
Azoxystrobin — systemic strobilurin for curative control
eco_friendly_treatment: Trichoderma viride soil application — biocontrol agent against soil-borne inoculum
Neem cake application (250 kg/ha) — soil amendment reducing fungal survival
Pseudomonas fluorescens foliar spray — induces systemic resistance in plant
Wood ash dusting — provides potassium and creates alkaline surface unfavorable for fungus
Whey spray (diluted 1:10) — natural lactic acid inhibits fungal growth
Potato___Early_blight.nutritional_impact: Moderate — tuber starch content may decrease by 5-10% under heavy infection. Vitamin C content in tubers is not significantly affected. Yield reduction (10-30%) is the primary economic impact rather than nutritional quality change.
best_practices: Practice 2-3 year crop rotation with non-solanaceous crops
Remove and destroy crop debris after harvest
Maintain adequate nitrogen and potassium fertilization
Irrigate early in the day so foliage dries before evening
Apply first fungicide when disease symptoms appear on lower leaves
Hill up soil around plants to protect tubers from spore wash-down
prevention: Maintain proper plant spacing for air circulation

Tomato___Bacterial_spot.name: Bacterial Spot
Tomato___Bacterial_spot.description: Bacterial spot is a destructive disease of tomato causing small, dark, water-soaked lesions on leaves, stems, and fruit. It thrives in warm, humid conditions and spreads rapidly through rain splash, overhead irrigation, and contaminated seed.
causes: Bacterial pathogen (Xanthomonas) spread via contaminated seed or transplants
Rain splash and overhead irrigation dispersing bacteria
Warm, humid weather (24-30°C with frequent rain)
Wounds from wind, insects, or mechanical damage facilitating entry
Lack of copper-resistant management strategies
symptoms: Small, dark, water-soaked spots on leaves often with yellow halos
Lesions may coalesce causing large areas of leaf death
Raised, scab-like spots on green fruit
Fruit spotting reduces marketability
Severe defoliation exposing fruit to sunscald
chemical_treatment: Copper hydroxide — bactericidal, reduced efficacy if resistance present
Streptomycin (where permitted) — antibiotic for early-season control
Mancozeb + copper tank mix — enhanced protection
eco_friendly_treatment: Bacillus subtilis QST 713 (Serenade) — OMRI-listed biocontrol
Acibenzolar-S-methyl (plant defense activator) — induces systemic resistance
Copper sulfate in dilute Bordeaux mixture — traditional organic option
Neem oil + baking soda spray — reduces bacterial load on foliage
Garlic-chili extract spray — natural antimicrobial barrier
Tomato___Bacterial_spot.nutritional_impact: Low — fruit with superficial spotting is safe to eat with normal nutritional content. Lycopene and vitamin C levels are not affected in non-necrotic fruit tissue. Severe infections cause yield loss rather than nutritional degradation.
best_practices: Use certified pathogen-free seed or hot-water treated seed (50°C for 25 min)
Avoid working in fields when foliage is wet
Use drip irrigation instead of overhead sprinklers
Rotate out of solanaceous crops for 2+ years
Apply copper sprays preventively in warm, wet weather
Remove and destroy severely infected plants
prevention: Purchase disease-free transplants from reputable sources
Disinfect tools, trays, and greenhouse structures
Space plants adequately for air movement

Tomato___Early_blight.name: Early Blight
Tomato___Early_blight.description: Early blight of tomato is among the most common and damaging foliar diseases globally. It produces characteristic dark, concentric-ring 'bull's eye' or 'target spot' lesions on lower foliage and can infect stems, fruit, and seedlings.
causes: Fungal spores surviving in infected plant debris and soil for up to 2 years
Warm humid conditions (24-29°C with high moisture)
Nutritional stress, particularly nitrogen deficiency
Aging or senescent lower leaves being most susceptible
Consecutive tomato or potato cropping in same soil
symptoms: Dark brown spots with concentric rings (target spots) starting on lower leaves
Yellow halo surrounding each lesion
Leaf yellowing and premature drop, progressing upward
Dark leathery spots at stem end of fruit
Collar rot on seedling stems near soil line
chemical_treatment: Chlorothalonil — protectant fungicide, apply before infection
Mancozeb — contact fungicide for preventive control
Azoxystrobin + difenoconazole — systemic combination for curative action
eco_friendly_treatment: Neem oil foliar spray (2-3%) — inhibits spore germination
Trichoderma harzianum soil and foliar application — biocontrol antagonist
Pseudomonas fluorescens seed treatment and foliar spray — induces systemic resistance
Cow urine spray (10% dilution) — traditional antifungal used in organic farming
Baking soda (sodium bicarbonate 0.5%) spray — creates alkaline surface inhibiting fungal growth
Tomato___Early_blight.nutritional_impact: Low to moderate — lycopene content in infected fruit may decrease by 8-12%. Vitamin C is largely unaffected in non-necrotic tissue. Fruit from defoliated plants may have lower sugar content due to reduced photosynthesis.
best_practices: Mulch around plants to prevent soil splash carrying spores to leaves
Stake or cage plants for better air circulation and reduced soil contact
Water at the base of plants; avoid wetting foliage
Remove lower leaves that show symptoms immediately
Practice 3-year rotation with non-solanaceous crops
Apply preventive fungicide before rainy periods
prevention: Choose tolerant varieties (Mountain Magic, Iron Lady)
Apply thick organic mulch (straw, wood chips)

Tomato___Late_blight.name: Late Blight
Tomato___Late_blight.description: Late blight is one of the most destructive plant diseases in history — it caused the Irish Potato Famine. It spreads explosively in cool, wet weather and can destroy an entire field within days. The pathogen produces sporangia that travel by wind and rain.
causes: Oomycete (Phytophthora infestans) spores from infected plants, volunteer tomatoes, or potatoes
Cool, wet weather (10-20°C, >90% humidity) ideal for rapid spread
Wind carrying sporangia over long distances
Infected transplants or seed potatoes introducing the pathogen
Dense plantings and poor air circulation
symptoms: Large, dark green to brown water-soaked lesions on leaves
White fuzzy mold growth on underside of leaves in humid conditions
Rapidly expanding brown/black areas on stems
Firm, brown, greasy-looking lesions on fruit
Entire plant can collapse within 7-10 days
chemical_treatment: Metalaxyl/Mefenoxam — systemic, highly effective against oomycetes
Chlorothalonil — protectant, requires preventive application
Cymoxanil + mancozeb — combination with curative and protectant action
eco_friendly_treatment: Bordeaux mixture (1%) — copper-based organic-approved fungicide
Copper hydroxide spray — effective protectant for organic production
Trichoderma viride + Pseudomonas fluorescens — dual biocontrol agents
Neem oil spray (3%) — some suppressive activity against sporangia
Milk spray (10-40% solution) — some efficacy reported in research trials
Tomato___Late_blight.nutritional_impact: High — infected fruit is inedible and must be discarded. Healthy fruit from partially affected plants retains normal nutritional value. The primary impact is total crop loss rather than nutritional degradation.
best_practices: Monitor fields daily during cool, wet weather
Remove and destroy infected plants immediately — do not compost
Apply protectant fungicide BEFORE disease appears during favorable weather
Use varieties with late blight resistance (Defiant, Mountain Merit, Legend)
Avoid overhead irrigation; water at plant base
Destroy all volunteer tomato and potato plants in the area
prevention: Monitor late blight forecasting systems (USAblight.org equivalents)
Apply preventive treatments during cool, wet periods

Tomato___Leaf_Mold.name: Leaf Mold
Tomato___Leaf_Mold.description: Tomato leaf mold is primarily a greenhouse and high-tunnel disease favored by high humidity and poor ventilation. It produces pale green to yellow spots on upper leaf surfaces with olive-green to grayish-purple velvety growth underneath.
causes: High relative humidity (>85%) and poor air ventilation
Temperatures of 22-25°C with condensation on leaf surfaces
Greenhouse or tunnel environments with inadequate ventilation
Dense planting and insufficient pruning
Spores persisting on greenhouse structures and stakes
symptoms: Pale green to yellowish diffuse spots on upper leaf surface
Olive-green to grayish-brown velvety mold on lower leaf surface
Leaves curl upward and eventually wither
Infection progresses from lower to upper canopy
Rarely affects fruit directly, but severe defoliation reduces yield
chemical_treatment: Azoxystrobin — systemic strobilurin fungicide
Difenoconazole — triazole fungicide for curative action
Chlorothalonil — protectant application in field settings
eco_friendly_treatment: Improve ventilation — the most effective single intervention
Bacillus subtilis foliar spray — biocontrol colonizer
Potassium bicarbonate spray — disrupts fungal growth
Neem oil (2%) — reduces sporulation on treated surfaces
Silicon foliar spray — strengthens leaf cell walls and cuticle
Tomato___Leaf_Mold.nutritional_impact: Low — leaf mold rarely directly infects fruit. However, severe defoliation reduces photosynthetic capacity, potentially lowering sugar content (Brix) by 5-8% in harvested fruit. Lycopene and vitamin content remain normal.
best_practices: Reduce humidity below 85% through ventilation and heating
Space plants and prune to allow air movement through canopy
Avoid wetting foliage — use drip irrigation only
Remove older infected leaves and dispose outside the growing area
Disinfect greenhouse structures, stakes, and clips between seasons
Grow resistant varieties (many modern hybrids carry Cf resistance genes)
prevention: Clean and sanitize greenhouse between crops

Rice___Brown_spot.name: Brown Spot
Rice___Brown_spot.description: Rice brown spot is a significant disease historically associated with the Bengal Famine of 1943. It causes oval to circular brown spots on leaves and can also infect grain, reducing both yield and grain quality. It is strongly associated with nutrient-deficient soils.
causes: Fungal spores from infected seeds, crop debris, and wild grasses
Nutrient-deficient soils, especially lacking potassium and silicon
Drought stress or inconsistent water management
Temperature range of 25-30°C with high humidity
Use of infected, untreated seed
symptoms: Oval to circular brown spots on leaves with gray or white centers
Spots have dark brown margins
Spots on leaf sheaths and panicles
Infected grain shows brown discoloration and reduced quality
Severe cases lead to unfilled or shriveled grains
affected_plants: Rice (all varieties, upland rice most susceptible)
chemical_treatment: Mancozeb — protective foliar spray
Carbendazim — systemic fungicide, seed treatment and foliar spray
Propiconazole — triazole fungicide for active disease control
eco_friendly_treatment: Trichoderma viride seed treatment (4g/kg seed) — reduces seed-borne inoculum
Pseudomonas fluorescens seed and foliar application — induces plant resistance
Neem cake soil application (150 kg/ha) — improves soil health and suppresses pathogen
Silicon fertilization (calcium silicate) — strengthens cell walls against fungal penetration
Panchagavya spray (3%) — traditional organic preparation enhancing plant immunity
Rice___Brown_spot.nutritional_impact: Moderate — infected grains show 10-20% reduction in starch content and lower milling quality. Protein content may be slightly elevated in surviving grains (stress response). Iron and zinc micronutrient content is generally unaffected.
best_practices: Use balanced fertilization — ensure adequate potassium, phosphorus, and silicon
Treat seeds with fungicide or biocontrol agent before sowing
Maintain consistent water management — avoid drought stress
Remove and destroy crop residues after harvest
Use certified disease-free seed
Apply foliar spray at tillering and boot stages if disease pressure is high
prevention: Seed treatment with Trichoderma or carbendazim
Maintain proper water levels in paddy

healthy_response.description: No disease detected. The plant appears healthy based on the visual analysis. Continue monitoring for early signs of disease and maintain good agricultural practices.
best_practices: Continue regular crop monitoring at least twice a week
Maintain proper irrigation and drainage
Follow recommended fertilization schedule
Practice crop rotation to prevent disease buildup
Use certified disease-free planting material
Maintain proper spacing for air circulation
Remove weeds that may harbor pests and diseases
